fed_monetary: The Fed holds the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for a second consecutive meeting, with FOMC officials signaling just one cut in 2026 as Iran war-driven oil supply disruptions threaten to re-accelerate an already-elevated inflation path. PPI running at 6.03% versus a 2.6% estimate, core PCE at 2.97% (above the 2.8% target), and 1-year Cleveland Fed inflation expectations at 3.26% collectively preclude any near-term easing; St. Louis Fed President Musalem stated policy is 'well positioned to address risks to both dual mandate objectives.' The Fed's balance sheet (WALCL $6.71T) continues passive runoff, while net U.S. liquidity of $5.95T and reserves of $3.13T keep systemic funding conditions functional, though the composite liquidity score at only the 37th percentile warrants monitoring.
labor_data: The March labor market print was unambiguously resilient: NFP surged +178K against a +100K consensus, unemployment held at 4.3% in-line with estimates, and weekly initial claims of 207K came in below the 210K forecast. ADP data through late March showed a third consecutive week of private hiring acceleration, while marginally attached workers ticked up 325K — a potential leading indicator of softening at the edges. The labor market remains the principal argument against aggressive easing, providing the Fed with political cover to hold rates even as energy-driven cost pressures squeeze real incomes.
fiscal_supply: Fiscal dynamics are deteriorating at an accelerating pace: CBO reports the Treasury borrowed $1 trillion in the first five months of FY2026 at roughly $50 billion per week, with March's adjusted deficit running $249 billion — 2% wider year-over-year. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) is projected to add $4.2 trillion to cumulative deficits through FY2034, while a series of weak Treasury auctions in March raised demand-absorption concerns. With TGA at $751.4B and RRP near zero ($0.5B), the Treasury's near-term buffer remains intact, but the structural supply overhang at elevated yields (10Y at 4.26%) represents an ongoing term-premium headwind.
corporate: Credit markets reflect cautious optimism tempered by macro cross-currents: HY OAS at 287 bps and IG OAS at 101 bps are tight by historical standards, suggesting investors are not yet pricing recession risk, though credit spread widening is being flagged as an early-warning indicator by institutional managers. WTI crude's 3.89% daily surge to $90.05 — with Hormuz closure scenarios projecting $110–$132/bbl if the disruption extends beyond one quarter — presents a material margin headwind for energy-intensive sectors. VIX at 19.95, just below the 20 threshold, and SPX -0.33% on the session reflect a market that is increasingly vigilant but not yet in distress.
next_catalyst: The single most critical near-term event is the BEA's Q1 2026 advance GDP estimate scheduled for April 30, which will either validate or undermine the soft-landing narrative by quantifying whether the economy absorbed tighter financial conditions and geopolitical energy shocks without material growth deceleration.
sources: Fed holds rate at 3.50–3.75%; Powell signals one 2026 cut as Iran war threatens to worsen inflation — PBS News/AP
IEA: Global oil supply plunges 10.1 mb/d to 97 mb/d in March as Iran war and Hormuz closure trigger largest supply shock since the 1970s — IEA Oil Market Report, April 2026
CBO: Treasury borrowed $1 trillion in five months of FY2026 at ~$50B/week; March adjusted deficit $249B, +2% YoY — Fortune / Bipartisan Policy Center
OBBBA projected to add $4.2 trillion to cumulative deficits through FY2034; weak Treasury auctions in March raise absorption concerns — Bipartisan Policy Center
Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate scheduled for April 30, 2026 — Polymarket / BEA Release Schedule
